access.reasoning: The repository record (src_id e2, OpenNeuro dataset record) explicitly states 'License: CC0'. CC0 is a public domain dedication placing no restrictions on use; combined with OpenNeuro's standard practice of direct download links on the dataset page, this is classified as open access (no account, no approval step required).

queries_run: OpenAlex: inference testing densely sampled single-subject task fMRI
EuropePMC: densely sampled single subject task fMRI inference permutation
EuropePMC core fetch: DOI:10.64898/2026.06.29.735344 (dataset descriptor abstract)
EuropePMC: single subject task fMRI statistical inference parametric permutation reproducibility
novelty.still_open_because: The dataset's own descriptor paper (Medina, Reddy, Bright, Sitek, 2026; DOI 10.64898/2026.06.29.735344, titled identically to the dataset) IS the study that answers this exact question. It collected whole-brain multi-echo densely sampled auditory task fMRI and explicitly "evaluated activation sensitivity as well as activation stability across data subsets" comparing voxel-level, cluster-level, and non-parametric permutation inference schemes. Its central finding directly resolves the generalization question: a whole-brain voxel-level FDR (p<0.05) approach gave the highest sensitivity and most consistent auditory-region detection "across regions and subjects" — i.e., one inference approach generalizes across subjects rather than each subject needing a different method. The proposed question is not novel; it restates the primary aim and result of the paper that produced the dataset.

queries_run: EuropePMC: densely sampled single-subject task fMRI inference (found dataset paper, Medina et al. 2026)
EuropePMC: single-subject task fMRI habituation activation across repeated runs drift
EuropePMC: densely sampled precision fMRI run-to-run activation change learning habituation threshold
EuropePMC: task fMRI temporal drift activation magnitude longitudinal change-point single subject
EuropePMC: auditory cortex fMRI habituation repeated task runs activation decrease
EuropePMC: precision fMRI task activation nonstationarity session order effect run number
novelty.still_open_because: The identified dataset paper (Medina et al. 2026, DOI 10.64898/2026.06.29.735344) is a methods paper evaluating statistical inference-testing approaches for this data; it does not model run-order drift. No located work applies a longitudinal or change-point model to detect systematic directional drift (habituation/learning/fatigue) in per-subject task-audio activation magnitude across the ordered run sequence, nor tests whether including such a drift term changes which regions survive thresholding. Adjacent work exists only on task-fMRI reliability/precision (e.g. 2024 precision-neuroscience reliability of emotion-processing responses), which concerns measurement reliability rather than systematic run-order drift and its downstream effect on statistical thresholding.

novelty.still_open_because: No prior work applies a publicly available pretrained self-supervised fMRI/BOLD foundation model with few-shot fine-tuning to this densely-sampled single-subject task-audio dataset, nor benchmarks it against from-scratch per-subject classifiers to quantify how much dense per-subject sampling a foundation model can substitute for. The source dataset paper (10.64898/2026.06.29.735344) concerns statistical inference-testing approaches only; existing fMRI foundation-model and few-shot-decoding work does not target this precision/dense single-subject substitution question.

EuropePMC: TMS target individualized fMRI localization reliability reproducibility
EuropePMC: activation peak coordinate convergence millimeter scan duration individual fMRI targeting
EuropePMC: how many runs stabilize individual functional target neuromodulation fMRI reliability
novelty.still_open_because: The dataset's own source paper (Evaluating Approaches for Inference Testing of Whole-Brain Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data, DOI 10.64898/2026.06.29.735344) already analyzes the same 5-subject auditory dense-mapping data and explicitly evaluates 'activation stability across data subsets,' reporting consistent detection 'even with lower scan duration' — the adjacent stability-vs-data-amount analysis. However, its stability metric quantifies detection sensitivity/consistency of competing statistical inference schemes (voxel-level FDR, cluster-level, permutation, top-% t-stat), NOT the specific quantity the question asks for: how many accumulated runs are required for each subject's peak auditory-cortex activation COORDINATE to converge spatially within a clinically usable tolerance (<5mm) for neuromodulation/TMS targeting, and whether that per-subject run-count differs meaningfully. The prior paper does not report a per-subject minimum-run-count-to-stabilize in millimeters framed for TMS targeting. That precise translational analysis remains open, though it builds directly on the existing stability-vs-subset framework.

feasibility.reason:
Both claimed variables check out against e2: "Modalities: mri" + "Tasks: audio" confirms task-audio fMRI runs exist, and "Number of subjects: 5" plus the dataset's own title ("...Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data") confirms 5 subjects each densely sampled on the same audio task. 334 total files / 5 subjects (~67 files/subject) and 10.9GB total is consistent with many runs per subject, not just one scan each, so "densely sampled" is plausible, not just asserted. The n=5 is NOT a feasibility problem here — the analysis unit is single-subject inference (does the parametric-vs-permutation choice generalize within each of 5 individual subjects), which is exactly the precision-fMRI/dense-sampling paradigm this dataset was purpose-built for (title literally says "Evaluating Approaches for Inference Testing"). No claimed variable is absent from the evidence, and CC0 licensing + public OpenNeuro/S3 hosting means no access-latency risk.

However, the claimed difficulty (3) and effort (8 weeks) look optimistic for what the analysis actually requires: (1) a full BIDS-based fMRI preprocessing pipeline (motion correction, registration, nuisance regression) run per-subject per-run; (2) building and fitting parametric first-level GLMs (e.g., FSL FEAT/SPM) with cluster-based multiple-comparison correction; (3) implementing and running a separate nonparametric/permutation inference stream (e.g., FSL randomise or equivalent) on the same data; (4) a defined reproducibility metric (e.g., Dice overlap, run-to-run map correlation) applied within-subject across dense runs, then compared across all 5 subjects to answer the generalization question. None of this is exotic, but it assumes working neuroimaging-pipeline fluency (FSL/AFNI/SPM, BIDS, cluster correction theory, permutation testing) that a student without prior fMRI analysis experience will need real ramp-up time for, plus non-trivial compute/wall-clock time per subject given "densely sampled" run counts. I raised difficulty to 4/5 and effort to ~11 weeks to leave room for that learning curve and iteration, while still fitting inside a 15-week semester.

missing_variables: Per-subject BOLD run count and acquisition order (e2 only gives an aggregate 'Total files: 334' across all 5 subjects and all file types (anat/fieldmap/physio/sidecars, not just audio-task BOLD runs); no per-subject breakdown or run numbering is present in the fetched evidence)
Session structure per subject ('Sessions:' field in e2 is blank/empty, so multi-session vs single-session dense sampling is unconfirmed)
Actual file listing / directory tree (source e1, the landing_page fetch, returned 0 chars — the one source that could confirm file-naming/run structure failed to retrieve)
Task design / event-timing files (events.tsv) needed to build per-run GLMs for the audio task — not confirmed present

Acquisition parameters (TR, run duration, number of volumes) needed to assess whether per-run activation magnitude estimates are stable/comparable enough for change-point modeling — not present in evidence
feasibility.reason: Only 2 of the evidence snippets exist and one (e1, the file listing) failed to fetch (0 chars), so the claim that '334 total files implies many runs per subject' is an inference from an aggregate count, not a confirmed per-subject run/session structure. The 5-subject densely-sampled single-subject design itself is confirmed. The proposed analysis is conceptually sound for this dataset genre (MSC-style dense sampling naturally supports within-subject drift/change-point analysis, and N=5 is fine since the unit of analysis is each subject's own run sequence), but it requires a full preprocessing pipeline (fMRIPrep on ~11GB), per-run first-level GLMs, longitudinal/change-point modeling, and re-thresholded whole-brain map comparisons per subject — a heavier scope than the claimed 7 weeks, especially with pipeline setup/debugging risk and unverified run/session metadata. Downgraded from difficulty 3/7 weeks to difficulty 4/10 weeks and marked FEASIBLE_WITH_CAVEAT pending direct verification of per-subject run counts before committing to the design.

missing_variables: Explicit run count per subject (evidence gives only 'Total files: 334' and 'Size bytes: 10.9GB' for 5 subjects — no stated number of runs/subject, so 'multiple runs per subject' is inferred from the title's 'Densely Sampled' phrase, not stated directly)

Task-block vs. rest design structure (evidence confirms 'Tasks: audio' but no events.tsv/paradigm description confirming a block design with rest periods — needed for the exact task-block-vs-rest decoding target)

Preprocessed/derivative data (evidence shows only raw BIDS totals, no mention of fMRIPrep or other derivatives — student must run a full preprocessing pipeline before any decoding or foundation-model input can be constructed)

Publicly available pretrained fMRI/BOLD self-supervised foundation model compatible with this dataset's acquisition parameters (external dependency not addressed by the evidence at all; such models are few, often require specific atlases/TR/preprocessing pipelines that may not match this newly-released dataset)
feasibility.reason: The two claimed variables are weakly-to-moderately supported: "Number of subjects: 5" and the title's "Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data" support the 5-subject dense-sampling design, and "Modalities: mri / Tasks: audio" supports audio-task BOLD data existing. But nothing in the 750-char record states run counts per subject, confirms a task-block/rest paradigm structure, or indicates preprocessed derivatives are included — the dataset appears to be raw BIDS only (10.9GB/334 files, no derivatives mentioned), so a student must build a full fMRIPrep-class preprocessing pipeline before any decoding is possible. Beyond the dataset itself, the project's core requirement — a publicly available pretrained fMRI/BOLD self-supervised foundation model — is an external dependency not evidenced at all; such models are scarce, typically expect specific atlases/TR/denoising pipelines, and adapting one to a brand-new, non-standard densely-sampled audio dataset (published as a bioRxiv preprint dated 2026-06-29, i.e., essentially unstudied) is a substantial, unproven engineering task with real risk of incompatibility. Combined with preprocessing burden, few-shot vs. full-data experimental design rigor, and only 5 subjects (single-subject framing mitigates but doesn't eliminate the small-N concern), this is more realistically a 13-15 week project for a strong student, not a 10-week one, and the claimed difficulty of 4 understates the foundation-model sourcing/adaptation and preprocessing risk. It can still fit a 15-week semester but only with little slack for debugging — hence FEASIBLE_WITH_CAVEAT rather than a clean FEASIBLE.

missing_variables: Per-subject / per-run breakdown of the task-audio runs: e2 only gives aggregate counts (Number of subjects: 5, Total files: 334) and 'Tasks: audio' — it does not state how many task-audio runs exist per subject, so the 'densely sampled, many runs per subject' premise is inferred only from the dataset TITLE ('Whole-Brain Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data'), not confirmed from a file listing or participants/scans table.
Task design/contrast details (events.tsv conditions, auditory stimulus timing) needed to define an 'auditory cortex activation' contrast per run — not present in the evidence record.
feasibility.reason: Evidence e2 confirms the two literally-claimed variables (5 subjects; modality=mri, task=audio) and the dataset's stated design intent ('Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI') strongly implies many runs per subject, which is plausible and likely correct. However, the evidence file provided does not itself show a per-subject run count (only an aggregate 334-file total across 5 subjects) or any task/events design info, so the core quantitative premise of the analysis ('many densely-sampled task-audio runs' per subject) is not directly verifiable from what was fetched — a student should confirm the actual per-subject run count (e.g., via the file tree or participants/scans.tsv) before committing to the project. Assuming that checks out, the analysis itself (per-run first-level GLM, peak auditory-cortex coordinate extraction in a common space, run-accumulation/order-resampling stability curves per subject, cross-subject comparison, single-run SNR concerns) is a nontrivial precision-fMRI pipeline requiring preprocessing (e.g., fMRIPrep), GLM expertise, and custom resampling analysis — more realistically ~10 weeks at difficulty 4/5 for a student than the claimed 3/8, though it fits within a 15-week semester and the data (10.9GB, CC0, immediately downloadable from OpenNeuro) has no access-latency barrier.

short_desc_es.value: Un conjunto de datos de OpenNeuro publicado bajo licencia CC0 con fMRI de tarea (tarea de audio) muestreada densamente en 5 sujetos individuales de cerebro completo, usado para evaluar enfoques de pruebas de inferencia sobre este tipo de datos.

scope.reason: Human whole-brain fMRI dataset (N=5, task-based audio paradigm) published on OpenNeuro in BIDS-compliant format. Directly suitable for neuroimaging research and training. Standard approved modality (fMRI) for neuroscience applications.

literature.method: Resolved DOI to OpenAlex work id W7166847277 (cited_by_count: 0, no results from cites:W7166847277 filter query — OpenAlex citation index confirms zero citing works). Cross-checked via Europe PMC search on dataset name and on 'ds008070' identifier: only the originating bioRxiv preprint itself was found (citedByCount: 0), no downstream citing works.
literature.notes: This dataset/preprint was published extremely recently (bioRxiv posted 2026-06-30; OpenNeuro DOI created 2026-07-01/02), only ~3 weeks before this analysis (today: 2026-07-21). No citing works exist yet in OpenAlex or Europe PMC. This is a brand-new resource with essentially no downstream literature to map — saturation is low simply because there has not been time for anyone to use it, not because the space has been explored and found empty. Re-check in 6-12 months for actual uptake.

projects.compute: Standard workstation; ~11 GB dataset download; no GPU required, though several hours of CPU time per subject for GLM plus permutation testing
projects.question_en: This dataset was built specifically to evaluate approaches for inference testing in single-subject task fMRI. Does the choice of statistical inference method (e.g., a parametric voxel-wise threshold vs. a permutation-based test) for detecting task-audio activation in one of these 5 subjects' densely-sampled runs generalize to the other 4 subjects, or does each subject require a different inference approach to reach a stable, reproducible activation map?
projects.question_es: Este dataset fue construido específicamente para evaluar enfoques de inferencia estadística en fMRI de tarea de un solo sujeto. ¿La elección del método de inferencia estadística (p. ej., un umbral paramétrico por vóxel frente a una prueba basada en permutaciones) para detectar activación en la tarea auditiva en uno de estos 5 sujetos con muestreo denso de corridas se generaliza a los otros 4 sujetos, o cada sujeto requiere un enfoque de inferencia distinto para lograr un mapa de activación estable y reproducible?

required_variables.variable: Task-audio functional MRI (BOLD) runs, mri modality
5 subjects, each densely sampled with multiple runs of the same audio task
required_variables.evidence: "Number of subjects: 5" ... "Name: Evaluating Approaches for Inference Testing of Whole-Brain Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data"

projects.still_open_because: The dataset's own title states it was built to evaluate inference-testing approaches, but the released repository record gives no indication that cross-subject generalizability of the winning inference method has itself been tested against the dataset's other subjects — that comparison is a natural, still-open student-scale question.

projects.question_en: Because each of the 5 subjects contributed many repeated runs of the same audio task (a 'densely sampled' single-subject design), can a simple longitudinal or change-point model detect a systematic drift (habituation, learning, or fatigue) in each subject's task-audio activation magnitude across their sequence of runs, and does modeling that drift change which brain regions survive statistical thresholding?
projects.question_es: Dado que cada uno de los 5 sujetos aportó muchas corridas repetidas de la misma tarea auditiva (un diseño de muestreo denso en un solo sujeto), ¿puede un modelo longitudinal o de punto de cambio sencillo detectar una deriva sistemática (habituación, aprendizaje o fatiga) en la magnitud de activación a la tarea auditiva de cada sujeto a lo largo de su secuencia de corridas, y modelar esa deriva cambia qué regiones cerebrales sobreviven el umbral estadístico?

required_variables.variable: Task-audio BOLD runs in acquisition order, per subject (334 total files across 5 subjects implies many runs per subject)

required_variables.evidence: "Name: Evaluating Approaches for Inference Testing of Whole-Brain Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data" ... "Number of subjects: 5"

projects.still_open_because: The evidence shows a densely-sampled repeated-runs design purpose-built for inference-method testing, but nothing in the record indicates any published analysis of within-subject temporal drift across those repeated runs — the causal/longitudinal angle is untouched.

projects.compute: Single GPU (e.g., Colab Pro, >=16GB VRAM) for fine-tuning; ~11 GB dataset download
projects.question_en: Using a publicly available pretrained fMRI/BOLD self-supervised foundation model, can few-shot fine-tuning on only a handful of one subject's densely-sampled task-audio runs match the decoding accuracy (task-block vs. rest) of a classifier trained from scratch on that same subject's full run set, quantifying how much of this dataset's dense per-subject sampling a foundation model can substitute for?
projects.question_es: Usando un modelo de fundación auto-supervisado preentrenado y disponible públicamente para fMRI/BOLD, ¿puede el ajuste fino few-shot con solo un puñado de corridas de la tarea auditiva de un sujeto (dentro de su muestreo denso) igualar la precisión de decodificación (bloque de tarea vs. reposo) de un clasificador entrenado desde cero con el conjunto completo de corridas de ese mismo sujeto, cuantificando cuánto del muestreo denso por sujeto de este dataset puede sustituir un modelo de fundación?

required_variables.variable: 5-subject, densely-sampled (many-run) design enabling a few-shot vs. full-data comparison
required_variables.evidence: "Number of subjects: 5" ... "Whole-Brain Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data"

projects.still_open_because: No indication in the evidence that this newly released (created 2026-06-30) dataset has been used as a foundation-model few-shot benchmark; its dense per-subject sampling makes it a natural but so-far-unexploited testbed for that question.

projects.question_en: Precision functional mapping for individualized neuromodulation targeting (e.g., TMS) requires many minutes of stable, subject-specific task data. Using each of the 5 subjects' many densely-sampled task-audio runs, how many runs must be accumulated before that subject's peak auditory-cortex activation coordinate stabilizes within a clinically usable tolerance (e.g., <5mm), and does the number of runs needed differ meaningfully across the 5 subjects?
projects.question_es: El mapeo funcional de precisión para dirigir neuromodulación individualizada (p. ej., TMS) requiere muchos minutos de datos de tarea estables y específicos del sujeto. Usando las numerosas corridas de la tarea auditiva con muestreo denso de cada uno de los 5 sujetos, ¿cuántas corridas deben acumularse antes de que la coordenada de activación pico en la corteza auditiva de ese sujeto se estabilice dentro de una tolerancia clínicamente utilizable (p. ej., <5mm), y difiere de forma relevante el número de corridas necesarias entre los 5 sujetos?

required_variables.variable: Task-audio BOLD runs, multiple per subject (densely sampled)
required_variables.evidence: "Modalities: mri\nTasks: audio" ... "Whole-Brain Densely Sampled Single-Subject Task fMRI Data"

projects.still_open_because: The dense multi-run single-subject design is exactly the kind of data precision-targeting studies need, but the evidence record shows this dataset was built for inference-method evaluation, not for a target-stability/neuromodulation-readiness analysis, so that use is untested.
